data.hero_subheading: An industrial + CMF designer with a mechanical engineering background. I enjoy creating experiences that are fun, meaningful and user-centred.

children.text: I'm Josephine, an industrial and CMF designer with a mechanical engineering background based in the Netherlands. I believe great design lives at the intersection of function, material, and meaning.

My work spans concept design, CMF strategy, user research, and 3D visualisation. I enjoy working on projects where the physical and experiential qualities of a product are just as important as its technical performance.
Previously I've worked on urban furniture, consumer electronics, and sustainable mobility. I'm currently open to new opportunities — feel free to reach out.

data.title: Energy Tree
data.work_caption: Urban furniture concept for the 2030 energy transition
data.summary: Urban furniture concept designed to make the 2030 energy transition visible and participatory for citizens of Dordrecht.
data.description: Energy Tree is a solar-powered urban furniture concept that turns passive street infrastructure into an active touchpoint in Dordrecht’s sustainability story. Positioned at key pedestrian intersections, each unit harvests solar energy through an overhead canopy of photovoltaic panels shaped to evoke a tree canopy. USB charging ports and ambient LED lighting make the benefits of renewable energy immediately tangible to passersby, while a small display shows real-time energy generation data. The CMF strategy pairs powder-coated COR-TEN steel with recycled-content polycarbonate diffusers, giving the piece a warm, civic character that ages gracefully in a public setting.
data.project_tagline: ENERGY TREE — URBAN FURNITURE CONCEPT — ENCOURAGING BEHAVIOUR CHANGE TOWARDS A GREENER LIFESTYLE
data.home_overlay_text:
ENERGY TREE
Urban furniture concept design to promote the 2030 energy transition in Dordrecht

children.text: Design Brief

The Municipality of Dordrecht commissioned a concept for urban furniture that would make the 2030 energy transition legible and engaging at street level. The brief called for a piece that generates renewable energy, provides a public amenity, and opens a conversation about sustainable urban living — without resorting to didactic signage or green-washing aesthetics. The design needed to integrate into the existing streetscape language while standing out enough to invite curiosity.

The starting point was a simple question: what does the energy transition feel like at human scale? Most sustainability infrastructure — wind turbines, solar farms — operates at a distance from everyday life. Energy Tree was designed to close that gap.
Research & Context

Field research in Dordrecht revealed that residents understood the importance of the energy transition intellectually but felt disconnected from its progress. Interviews surfaced a consistent theme: people wanted to see and feel a contribution, not just read about targets. This shaped the core design principle — make energy generation immediate and personal.
CMF & Detail

The canopy structure uses a modular array of bifacial solar cells mounted in polycarbonate housings. COR-TEN steel was chosen for the primary structure for its self-healing patina — a material that visibly ages alongside the city. At night, harvested energy powers warm-white ambient lighting beneath the canopy, transforming the piece into a quiet lantern.

data.title: Meridian
data.summary: Complete visual identity system for Meridian Collective, a platform bridging contemporary art and emerging technology.
data.work_caption: Brand identity for a creative collective
data.description: Meridian Collective brings together artists and technologists for collaborative residencies and public programmes. The identity needed to hold two worlds in tension: the slow, material quality of studio practice and the fast, networked logic of digital culture. The solution centres on a custom wordmark where classical proportions meet a subtle optical illusion — the letterforms read differently depending on viewing distance, a metaphor for the Collective’s dual audience. A restricted palette of warm off-white, deep ink, and a single iridescent accent carries across print, screen, and spatial applications.
data.project_tagline: MERIDIAN — WHERE STUDIO PRACTICE MEETS DIGITAL CULTURE — AN IDENTITY IN TWO REGISTERS
data.home_overlay_text:
MERIDIAN
Brand identity for a creative collective bringing together artists and technologists.

children.text: Design Brief

Meridian Collective needed a visual identity flexible enough to live across gallery invitations, Instagram grids, exhibition catalogues, and digital interfaces — without feeling like it belonged to only one of those contexts. The brief specified no illustrative elements: typography and colour alone had to do all the work. The identity also needed to scale gracefully from a business card to a 6-metre exhibition banner.

The Meridian brief arrived with a mood board that contained no images — only words. “Timeless but not nostalgic. Digital but not cold. Collective but with a distinct voice.” That tension became the brief.
Wordmark Development

Over thirty wordmark explorations were reduced to three directions: a geometric sans, a modified classical serif, and a hybrid that drew from both. The hybrid won — its slightly flared terminals give warmth at small sizes while the strict cap-height geometry maintains authority at large scale.
Colour & Application

The iridescent accent — a foil in print, a CSS gradient on screen — appears only on the most significant touchpoints: the cover of the annual programme, the header of the website, the stamp on exhibition collateral. Restraint makes each appearance feel deliberate.

data.title: Volta
data.summary: Website design and UX for Volta Energy, making residential solar accessible through clarity, trust, and a streamlined quote journey.
data.work_caption: Website design for a renewable energy startup
data.description: Volta Energy’s existing website buried the most important action — getting a quote — behind four pages of technical content. The redesign inverted that logic: the quote journey begins on the homepage, with educational content available as a secondary path for visitors who want to understand before they commit. A warm green and natural stone palette replaced the previous corporate blues, positioning Volta as a trusted neighbourhood company rather than a faceless utility. Every page was tested with first-time solar buyers to ensure the language stayed human.
data.project_tagline: VOLTA — MAKING CLEAN ENERGY FEEL LIKE AN OBVIOUS CHOICE — DESIGN FOR TRUST AT SCALE
data.home_overlay_text:
VOLTA
Website design for a renewable energy startup making clean power accessible to everyone.

children.text: Design Brief

Volta Energy had strong technology and competitive pricing but a website that its own sales team described as "too engineering-focused." The brief asked for a redesign that would reduce the average time-to-quote from 6 minutes to under 2, increase conversion on mobile (where 68% of traffic arrived), and establish Volta as the most approachable brand in the Dutch residential solar market.

The first research session revealed the core problem: visitors arrived motivated but left confused. The existing site answered questions nobody had asked while leaving the obvious ones — “How much will it cost?” and “How long does installation take?” — buried in an FAQ accordion.
UX Strategy

We mapped every question a first-time buyer has across a six-week consideration journey and structured the site architecture around those questions in chronological order. The homepage speaks to day-one curiosity. Product pages speak to week-two comparison. The quote flow speaks to week-six readiness.
Visual Language

The green accent was derived from the specific wavelength of light that chlorophyll absorbs most efficiently — a nerdy detail that nobody notices consciously but that gives the colour an unusual naturalness. Paired with warm greige backgrounds and generous whitespace, it reads as calm and confident rather than aggressively ecological.

data.title: The Archive
data.summary: Print identity for The Archive — a 240-page exhibition catalogue and complete wayfinding system for a retrospective on 50 years of modernist architecture.
data.description: The Archive retrospective brought together 200 works spanning five decades of modernist architecture from across Europe. The print identity had to honour the rigour of the subject without becoming a pastiche of mid-century graphic design. The catalogue uses a 12-column grid derived directly from the proportional system used by the exhibition’s most represented architect, Aldo van Eyck. Full-bleed archival photographs alternate with dense analytical spreads, creating a rhythm that mirrors the experience of moving through the exhibition itself. The typeface pairing — a narrow grotesque for body text and a wide-tracked geometric for headings — evokes technical drawing conventions without literal mimicry.
data.project_tagline: THE ARCHIVE — FIVE DECADES OF MODERNIST ARCHITECTURE — A RETROSPECTIVE IN PRINT

children.text: Design Brief

The City Museum required a complete print identity for The Archive: a 240-page hardcover catalogue, exhibition posters in three sizes, room-by-room wayfinding, and an accompanying zine for younger visitors. The catalogue needed to function both as an academic reference and as a desirable object in the museum shop. Budget constraints meant two-colour printing throughout, with the cover as the only four-colour exception.

Two-colour printing is not a limitation — it is a discipline. Every decision about what gets the second colour becomes a hierarchy decision. For The Archive, the second colour (a warm terracotta Pantone) was reserved for captions, section markers, and the architectural drawings. This single rule gave the entire catalogue its visual logic.
Grid System

The 12-column grid accommodates three distinct page types: full-bleed image spreads, 50/50 image-text splits, and dense text pages with marginal annotations. Transitions between types were choreographed across the catalogue like chapters in a film edit — breathing room before density, density before revelation.
Wayfinding

The exhibition wayfinding uses the same grid and typefaces as the catalogue, creating a visual continuity between the physical space and the printed object. Visitors who picked up the catalogue first could navigate the rooms with it; visitors who experienced the rooms first found the catalogue a familiar extension.

data.title: Coastal
data.summary: A year-long medium-format film series documenting the Pacific coastline at transitional light — dawn, dusk, and the hours in between.
data.work_caption: Photographic study of the Pacific coastline
data.description: Coastal began as a single weekend on the Oregon coast with a borrowed Mamiya 645 and ended twelve months later with 340 developed rolls and a shortlist of 48 images. The series is organised not by geography but by light condition: the blue hour before dawn, the hard side-light of morning, the flat midday diffusion, the golden collapse of late afternoon, and the long blue shadow of dusk. Within each condition, the Pacific reveals a different character — the same stretch of beach becomes five different places across a single day. The prints are made on a warm-tone fibre paper that holds shadow detail without sacrificing the luminosity of the horizon.
data.project_tagline: COASTAL — A YEAR OF LIGHT ON THE PACIFIC EDGE — MEDIUM FORMAT FILM 2022–2023

children.text: Design Brief

A self-initiated project with no client brief, which meant the brief had to be written retroactively from the work itself. After the first month of shooting it became clear that the project was not about the coast as a place but about the quality of attention that slow photography demands. The constraint: only shoot during the transitional hours. No midday frames. No artificial light. Every image had to be earned by being present at the right moment.

Film slows you down in a way that nothing else does. You have 15 frames per roll, you know exactly how many you have left, and each one costs money and time to develop. That economy of attention changes how you see — or rather, it restores a kind of seeing that digital abundance has quietly eroded.
Process

Each location was visited at least three times before any frames were exposed: once to understand the light, once to understand the tides, once to understand the people. By the time the camera came out, the image already existed — the shutter release was almost an afterthought.
Editing & Sequencing

The final sequence of 48 images was arrived at through six rounds of editing over four months. Images that were technically strong but emotionally neutral were cut. Images that were technically imperfect but held something — a quality of stillness, a particular quality of grey — stayed. The final sequence moves from dark to light and back to dark, a single day distilled from a year.
